questions.answer: C. Yes, mutual exclusion is ensured; but starvation is possible — a waiting process may never enter the critical section
questions.to_find: Whether the scheme ensures mutual exclusion, and whether a waiting process can be starved

understand.plain: Three processes Pi, Pj, Pk share a critical section. Each process sets its own flag before entering, and checks the flags of the other two. If another process has the turn and its flag is set, the current process temporarily lowers its flag and waits for the turn to rotate to itself. We must determine: (1) Is only one process ever in the critical section at a time? (2) Can a process wait forever?

keywords.explain: The guarantee that at most one process is inside the critical section at any given moment.
keywords.example: If Pi is in the critical section, neither Pj nor Pk can enter until Pi exits.

keywords.explain: A situation where a process is perpetually denied entry to the critical section even though it keeps trying.
keywords.example: If turn always oscillates between j and k, Pi may wait forever.

keywords.explain: A boolean array where flag[i] = true means process Pi wants to enter the critical section.
keywords.example: Pi sets flag[i] := true to announce its intention.

keywords.explain: A shared variable indicating which process has priority to proceed when there is contention.
keywords.example: If turn = j, then Pj has priority over Pi and Pk.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='140' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Pi</text><text x='80' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>flag[i]=true</text><rect x='170' y='10' width='140' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='240' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Pj</text><text x='240' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>turn=j (priority)</text><rect x='330' y='10' width='140' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='400' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Pk</text><text x='400' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>flag[k]=true</text><rect x='170' y='110' width='140' height='60' rx='8' class='ok'/><text x='240' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:white'>Critical</text><text x='240' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white'>Section</text><line x1='240' y1='70' x2='240' y2='110' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='80' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>Pi waits (starvation risk)</text><text x='400' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Pk also waits</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three processes Pi, Pj, Pk compete for the critical section. Pj has the turn, so it proceeds into the critical section while Pi lowers its flag and waits. If turn keeps alternating between j and k, Pi may starve.

given.aim: Determine whether the three-process critical section scheme guarantees mutual exclusion and is free from starvation.

terms.meaning: Boolean: process Pi wants to enter the critical section
terms.example: flag[i] := true means Pi is trying to enter
terms.connects: Used to detect contention; process lowers its flag when it defers

terms.meaning: Shared variable set to j or k to indicate whose turn has priority
terms.example: If turn = j, Pi defers to Pj when both want to enter
terms.connects: Drives the case statement inside the while loop

terms.meaning: Pi busy-waits as long as at least one of the other processes wants to enter
terms.example: Pi loops until both Pj and Pk lower their flags
terms.connects: Entry protocol that enforces single access

terms.meaning: On exit, Pi sets turn to either j or k (whichever is not already turn)
terms.example: if turn = j then turn := k else turn := j
terms.connects: This two-value alternation can exclude Pi if j and k keep alternating

given.plan: First verify mutual exclusion: check if two processes can simultaneously pass the while-loop guard. Then check for starvation: construct a scenario where Pi waits indefinitely while turn oscillates between j and k.

steps.title: Verify Mutual Exclusion
steps.formula_raw: At most one process has flag[x]=true AND has exited the while-loop at any time
steps.apply: Suppose Pi exits its while-loop (flag[j]=false AND flag[k]=false at that moment). For Pj to also exit its while-loop, it would need flag[i]=false and flag[k]=false. But Pi set flag[i]=true before checking, so Pj cannot exit its while-loop while Pi is past it. A symmetric argument holds for Pk. Hence at most one process is in the critical section at a time — mutual exclusion is guaranteed.
steps.note: The combination of flag[] checks and the temporary lowering (flag[i]:=false) does not break mutual exclusion because re-raising happens before re-checking.

steps.title: Check for Starvation
steps.formula_raw: turn alternates only between j and k; Pi's condition (turn = i) may never be satisfied
steps.apply: Consider: Pi sets flag[i]:=true and enters the while-loop. turn = j, so Pi lowers flag[i] and waits for turn != i (i.e., turn to become i, which it interprets as 'while turn != i do skip'). Now Pj enters and exits the critical section, setting turn := k. Pk then enters, exits, sets turn := j. This oscillation j->k->j->k can continue indefinitely. Pi's condition 'turn = i' is never set by Pj or Pk (they only toggle between j and k), so Pi may wait forever — starvation is possible. Fix: use a three-valued turn variable (i, j, k) with a round-robin update: after exit, turn := next(turn) cyclically.
steps.note: This is a classic starvation scenario caused by only two processes mutually granting each other priority while a third is ignored.

solution.result: Yes, mutual exclusion is ensured by the flag-based protocol. However, starvation is possible: Pi can wait indefinitely if turn alternates only between j and k, never reaching i. The turn variable must use a three-way round-robin to be starvation-free.

questions.formula_note: Peterson's algorithm generalization for three processes; starvation analysis via turn-variable round-robin argument.

questions.question:
Write a concurrent program using parbegin-parend and semaphores to represent the precedence constraints of the statements S_1 to S_n, as shown in the figure below.

The precedence graph (from the figure) shows the following dependency structure:
  S_1 -> S_2
  S_1 -> S_3
  S_2 -> S_4
  S_3 -> S_4
  S_4 -> S_5
  S_4 -> S_6
  S_5 -> S_7
  S_6 -> S_7

(Diamond/fork-join shaped DAG: S1 at top, S2 and S3 in parallel, S4 as join, then S5 and S6 in parallel, S7 as final join.)

Which of the following correctly captures the concurrent program with semaphores?

A. parbegin S1; S2; S3; S4; S5; S6; S7 parend
B. S1; parbegin S2; S3 parend; S4; parbegin S5; S6 parend; S7
C. S1; parbegin S2; S3 parend; parbegin S4; S5; S6 parend; S7
D. parbegin S1; parbegin S2; S3 parend; S4 parend; S7
questions.answer: B. S1; parbegin S2; S3 parend; S4; parbegin S5; S6 parend; S7
questions.to_find: The correct parbegin-parend concurrent program that captures the given precedence constraints

understand.plain: We have seven statements with a specific order of dependencies forming a diamond-within-diamond shape. S1 must run first, then S2 and S3 can run in parallel, then S4 must wait for both S2 and S3, then S5 and S6 run in parallel, and finally S7 must wait for both S5 and S6. We need to express this using parbegin-parend blocks.

keywords.explain: A programming construct that starts multiple statements in parallel (parbegin) and waits for all of them to finish (parend) before continuing.
keywords.example: parbegin S2; S3 parend means S2 and S3 execute concurrently; execution continues only when both finish.

keywords.explain: A directed acyclic graph (DAG) where an edge from Si to Sj means Sj can only start after Si finishes.
keywords.example: S1 -> S2 means S2 must wait for S1 to complete before it starts.

keywords.explain: A concurrency pattern where a single thread forks into multiple parallel threads (fork), then all threads join back to a single thread (join).
keywords.example: S1 forks into S2 and S3; they join at S4.

keywords.explain: A synchronization primitive (integer + wait/signal operations) used to coordinate ordering between concurrent processes.
keywords.example: After S2 finishes, it signals a semaphore; S4 waits on two semaphores before proceeding.

r='22' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='276' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S6</text><circle cx='170' cy='345' r='22' class='ok'/><text x='170' y='351' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:white'>S7</text><line x1='152' y1='48' x2='108' y2='91' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='188' y1='48' x2='232' y2='91' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='108' y1='129' x2='152' y2='171' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='232' y1='129' x2='188' y2='171' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='152' y1='208' x2='108' y2='251' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='188' y1='208' x2='232' y2='251' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='108' y1='289' x2='152' y2='326' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='232' y1='289' x2='188' y2='326' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A diamond-within-diamond DAG: S1 at top, forks to S2 (left) and S3 (right), both join at S4 (middle), S4 forks to S5 (left) and S6 (right), both join at S7 (bottom).

given.aim: Translate the precedence DAG into a correct parbegin-parend program that respects all ordering constraints.

terms.meaning: S1 must complete before either S2 or S3 can start; S2 and S3 have no dependency on each other
terms.example: After S1 finishes, both S2 and S3 can run simultaneously
terms.connects: Dictates a sequential S1 followed by parbegin S2; S3 parend

terms.meaning: S4 can only start after BOTH S2 and S3 have finished
terms.example: S4 is the join point of the first fork
terms.connects: The parend of the first parbegin block acts as the join, after which S4 runs sequentially

terms.meaning: S5 and S6 can only start after S4 finishes; they have no dependency on each other
terms.example: After S4 finishes, S5 and S6 run in parallel
terms.connects: Dictates a sequential S4 followed by parbegin S5; S6 parend

terms.meaning: S7 can only start after BOTH S5 and S6 have finished
terms.example: S7 is the final join point
terms.connects: The parend of the second parbegin block acts as the second join, after which S7 runs sequentially

given.plan: Read the DAG level by level. Identify sequential dependencies (single predecessor) and parallel sections (no dependency between siblings). Express each parallel section as a parbegin-parend block and chain them sequentially.

steps.title: Identify the sequential skeleton
steps.formula_raw: For any node with a single predecessor, it must run after that predecessor sequentially.
steps.apply: S1 has no predecessors -> runs first. S4 has two predecessors (S2, S3) -> must run after both finish. S7 has two predecessors (S5, S6) -> must run after both finish. So the top-level sequential order is: S1, [parallel block 1], S4, [parallel block 2], S7.
steps.note: The key insight is that S4 and S7 are 'join' nodes that force sequential execution after parallel blocks.

steps.title: Fill in the parallel blocks
steps.formula_raw: parbegin <stmt1>; <stmt2> parend runs stmt1 and stmt2 in parallel and waits for both.
steps.apply: Parallel block 1: S2 and S3 (both depend only on S1, no dependency between them) -> parbegin S2; S3 parend. Parallel block 2: S5 and S6 (both depend only on S4, no dependency between them) -> parbegin S5; S6 parend. Final program: S1; parbegin S2; S3 parend; S4; parbegin S5; S6 parend; S7. This is option B.
steps.note: Option C is wrong because it puts S4, S5, S6 all in parallel — but S4 must precede S5 and S6, so they cannot be parallel with S4.

steps.title: Verify with semaphores (for completeness)
steps.formula_raw: var s12, s13, s24, s34, s45, s46, s57, s67: semaphore := 0
steps.note: The parbegin-parend construct implicitly handles the synchronization at join points, so explicit semaphores are mainly needed when the construct is not available.

solution.result: The correct concurrent program is: S1; parbegin S2; S3 parend; S4; parbegin S5; S6 parend; S7 (Option B).

questions.formula_note: Fork-join concurrency pattern using parbegin-parend; precedence graph to parallel program translation.
